title: Aarthi Bharathan
text:
About Me
I am an undergraduate student majoring in Computational and Applied Mathematics and Statistics in Mathematical Biology (CAMS) and Data Science at William & Mary (Class of 2028). My interests include computational biology/genomics, biotechnology, and molecular genetics. I love learning about new genetic mechanisms that contribute to therapeutics. I hope to apply my research in understanding the genome evolution of various features in eukaryotes using computational techniques such as machine learning to a biotechnological setting where these features are attributes of new medicines.
Here you’ll find information about my research, outreach and education activities, and my comprehensive lab notebook documenting ongoing projects and findings.

text:
Spring 2026 goals

Sequencing: WGS, NGS, etc. are important skill that I want to grow at least by the end of my time in this lab ### Computational Work

Start HMMER (Transposase Project)

Cell imaging

title: Lab Notebook
text:
Welcome to my open lab notebook!
This is a cataloged and searchable collection of research notes and findings.

title: Lab Notebook
text:
How to Explore This Notebook

Search: Use the search box to find entries by keywords
Filter by Category: Click on categories to filter entries by project or topic
Sort: Sort entries by date, title, or other criteria
Browse: Scroll through all entries in chronological order

title: Week of March 1st Goals
text:
Week of March 1st




Compressed the data into 70% and 90% identity matches to filter and identify distant relatives to the reference sequence that contribute to the diversity in the lineage. This was done in today’s lab meeting. We made a python script and a bash script to compress all the fasta files that were stored in the query_dir directory on the HPC scr10 space. cd-hit was used to merge related sequences.

title: Spring Semester Goals
text:
Week of March 1st




I tried to make bar charts to visually see the distribution of the data and filter those with greater than 70% percent identity, but this was done without compressing the data.
Additionally, I read about domesticated transposases being an adaptation to evolutionary conflicts (Jangam et al.). The research paper emphasizes how transposable elements (TE) serve as defense mechanisms in both prokaryots and eukaryoets against invasive agents. There was an unconstrained evolution of DNA that led to their domestication. Overtime, these elements were disabled and integrated. Some resulting elements include retrotransposons and DNA transposable elements that use a cut and paste mechanism. Over time, long terminal repeats in retrotransposons led encoded GAG, POL, and ENV proteins. GAG is a capsid protein that encapsulates LTR mRNA into virus particles, ENV proteins are glycoproteins that promote virus particle escape, and POL proteins include integrase, RNAse, and RT. In short, transposases of various classes have been domesticated as an evolutionary advantage in suppressing other transposable elements and viruses because of preexisting interactions with the machinery and transposable elements themselves. This is regarded as a selfish strategy but also the primary reason behind domestication. The question that remains is was function facilitated by domesticated or was this a function that already existed as the element arrived to the host.
